title: Validation
text: The validation documentation and reports should cover the relevant steps of the life cycle. Manufacturers should be able to justify their standards, protocols, acceptance criteria, procedures and records based on their risk assessment. Validation documentation should include change control records (if applicable) and reports on any deviations observed during the validation process.

title: Audit Trails
text: Consideration should be given, based on a risk assessment, to building into the system the creation of a record of all GMP-relevant changes and deletions (a system generated 'audit trail'). For change or deletion of GMP-relevant data the reason should be documented. Audit trails need to be available and convertible to a generally intelligible form and regularly reviewed.

title: Security
text: Physical and/or logical controls should be in place to restrict access to computerised system to authorised persons. Suitable methods of preventing unauthorised entry to the system may include the use of keys, pass cards, personal codes with passwords, biometrics, restricted access to computer equipment and data storage areas.
Data should be attributable to the person or system creating the data. Records should show WHO acquired the data or performed the action and WHEN.
Data should be permanent and readable. Hand-written entries must be in indelible ink; electronic records should be in formats readable for the retention period.
Data should be recorded at the time the work is performed. Backdating, post-hoc reconstruction without audit trail, or batch-completion before work-done are violations.
Original data should be preserved. Where exact copies are required, the process to make those copies should be validated and the original should remain accessible.
Data should be true and reflect the observation/event. Corrections should preserve the original entry (strike-through with date+initials, not overwrite).
All data should be retained — including any reanalyses, repeated tests, or rejected results. Selective retention is a critical data-integrity violation.
Data should be consistent across the record (e.g. timestamps in sequence; lot numbers across documents match).
Data should be durable for the retention period — backup, disaster recovery, format migration where needed.
Data should be retrievable on demand for the retention period, for inspection by the regulatory authority.

title: PIC/S Good Practices for Data Management and Integrity
text: Detailed guidance on data lifecycle (creation through archive/disposal), data governance, role of QA in data integrity oversight, audit-trail review frequency and risk-based scope, common deficiencies (shared accounts, unrestricted blank record templates, manual transcription without verification, missing audit trails on critical systems).

title: FDA Data Integrity & Compliance with cGMP guidance
text: Q&A guidance addressing role of meta-data (audit trails are meta-data; loss of meta-data is loss of original record), backups vs archives (a backup is not an archive), dynamic vs static records (dynamic records must remain interactive), shared accounts (prohibited where individual accountability is required), validation of audit-trail functionality.
